annotations.label: Margin of error

anchor.snippet: The margin of error describes the distance within which a specified percentage of these results is expected to vary from

annotations.note: Margin of error is a survey-statistics concept with no corresponding definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Central limit theorem application

anchor.snippet: the margin of error helps to explain how the distribution of sample means

annotations.note: Mathlib has the strong law of large numbers (ProbabilityTheory.strong_law_ae) but no central limit theorem governing the distribution of sample means.

annotations.label: 68-95-99.7 rule for results

anchor.snippet: we would expect that 95% of the results

annotations.note: The empirical 68-95-99.7 rule (Gaussian mass within k standard deviations) is not stated or proved in Mathlib despite the Gaussian distribution being defined.

annotations.label: Confidence interval and margin of error

anchor.snippet: This interval is called the confidence interval , and the radius (half the interval) is called the margin of error

annotations.note: Confidence intervals are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General margin of error formula

anchor.snippet: has a margin of error

annotations.note: The margin-of-error formula (quantile times standard error) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lower quantile (z-score) and standard error

anchor.snippet: denotes the lower quantile (also, commonly, a z-score )

annotations.note: Mathlib defines CDFs (ProbabilityTheory.cdf) but has no quantile/inverse-CDF or z-score notion.

annotations.label: 95% confidence level computation

anchor.snippet: For example, using a confidence level of 95%, we would have

annotations.note: A worked numerical confidence-level computation has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard error

anchor.snippet: This is called the standard error

annotations.note: Standard error (standard deviation of the sample mean) is not defined in Mathlib, though variance/std of a single random variable is.

annotations.label: Variance of survey results

anchor.snippet: together would have a variance

annotations.note: The additivity used (variance of a sum of independent variables equals the sum of variances) is the general lemma IndepFun.variance_sum, but the survey/Bernoulli specialization itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Variance of a Bernoulli distribution

anchor.snippet: corresponds to the variance of a Bernoulli distribution

annotations.note: The Bernoulli distribution is defined (PMF.bernoulli) and the binomial variance np(1-p) appears only as a `proof_wanted` placeholder in Mathlib/Probability/Distributions/Binomial.lean, so Var = p(1-p) is not actually established as a real declaration.

annotations.label: Confidence interval about the mean

anchor.section: Maximum margin of error at different confidence levels
anchor.snippet: there is a corresponding confidence interval about the mean

annotations.note: Confidence intervals about the mean are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantile undefined at endpoints

anchor.section: Maximum margin of error at different confidence levels
anchor.snippet: is undefined for

annotations.note: No quantile function exists in Mathlib, so the claim about it being undefined at 0 and 1 has no formal analogue.

annotations.label: Maximum margin of error

anchor.section: Maximum margin of error at different confidence levels
anchor.snippet: to obtain the maximum margin of error for

annotations.note: The maximum margin of error formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Highest margin of error near 50%

anchor.snippet: the result closest to 50% will have the highest margin of error

annotations.note: The statement that p(1-p) (hence margin of error) is maximized near 50% is not formalized in the margin-of-error context.

annotations.label: Specific margins poll example

anchor.snippet: Imagine poll

annotations.note: A numerical polling example has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Margin of error at extremes

anchor.snippet: As a given percentage approaches the extremes of 0% or 100%, its margin of error approaches ±0%.

annotations.note: The limiting behavior of margin of error at 0% and 100% is not formalized.

annotations.label: Comparing percentages poll example

anchor.snippet: Imagine multiple-choice poll

annotations.note: A numerical multiple-choice polling example has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical tie

anchor.snippet: The popular notion of statistical tie or statistical dead heat

annotations.note: The notion of a statistical tie is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard error of difference (sum of variances)

anchor.snippet: we need to apply the sum of variances to obtain a new variance

annotations.note: Var[X - Y] = Var X - 2cov + Var Y is given by variance_sub (and variance_add with covariance), generalizing the variance-of-difference step, but the standard-error-of-difference statement is not itself formalized.

annotations.label: Covariance constancy assumption

anchor.snippet: is close to constant, that is, respondents choosing either A or B would almost never choose C

annotations.note: Covariance is defined in Mathlib, but the modeling assumption that it is approximately constant is not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Infinite population assumption

anchor.snippet: assume that there is an infinitely large population

annotations.note: This is a modeling assumption with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite population correction

anchor.snippet: analysts might adjust the margin of error using a finite population correction

annotations.note: The finite population correction factor (sampling without replacement) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite population correction example

anchor.snippet: were conducted over 24% of, say, an electorate of 300,000 voters

annotations.note: A numerical finite-population-correction example has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positivity of margin of error

anchor.snippet: The margin of error will be positive whenever a population is incompletely sampled and the outcome measure has positive variance

annotations.note: The general positivity criterion for margin of error (positive whenever sampling is incomplete and the measure has positive variance) is not formalized in Mathlib.
